volumeTitle: Trojan Women. Iphigenia among the Taurians. Ion
label: Ion (Section 1 - Section 2.220)

sections.label: Section 1
sections.translation: Atlas , who bears upon his brazen back the pressure of the sky , ancient dwelling of the gods , begat Maia from a daughter of one of those gods , and she bare me Hermes to mighty Zeus , to be the servant of the powers divine .

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: ἥκω δὲ Δελφῶν τήνδε γῆν , ἵνʼ ὀμφαλὸν
sections.translation: Lo ! I am come to this land of Delphi where sits Phoebus on the centre of the world and giveth oracles to men , ever chanting lays prophetic of things that are to be . Now there is a city in Hellas of no small note , called after Pallas , goddess of the golden lance ;

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: οὗ παῖδʼ Ἐρεχθέως Φοῖβος ἔζευξεν γάμοις
sections.translation: there did Phoebus force his love on Creusa , daughter of Erechtheus , beneath the rock of Pallas , northward of Athens ’ steep realm , called Macrae by the kings of Attica . And she without her father ’ s knowledge — for such was the god ’ s good pleasure ,—

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: γαστρὸς διήνεγκʼ ὄγκον . ὡς δʼ ἦλθεν χρόνος ,
sections.translation: bore the burden in her womb unto the end , and when her time came , she brought forth a child in the house and carried him away to the selfsame cave wherein the god declared his love to her , and she cradled him in the hollow of a rounded ark and cast him forth to die ,

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: προγόνων νόμον σῴζουσα τοῦ τε γηγενοῦς
sections.translation: observant of the custom of her ancestors and of earth-born Erichthonius , whom the daughter of Zeus gave into the charge of the daughters of Agraulus , after setting on either side , to keep him safe , a guard of serpents twain . Hence in that land among the Erechthidae ’ tis a

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: νόμος τις ἔστιν ὄφεσιν ἐν χρυσηλάτοις
sections.translation: custom to protect their babes with charms of golden snakes . But ere she left the babe to die , the young mother tied about him her own broidered robe . And this is the request that Phoebus craves of me , for he is my brother , Go , brother , to those children of the soil

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: κλεινῶν Ἀθηνῶν — οἶσθα γὰρ θεᾶς πόλιν —
sections.translation: that dwell in glorious Athens , for well thou knowest Athena ’ s city , and take a new-born babe from out the hollow rock , his cradle and his swaddling-clothes as well , and bear him to my prophetic shrine at Delphi , and set him at the entering-in of my temple .

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: τὰ δʼ ἄλλʼ — ἐμὸς γάρ ἐστιν , ὡς εἰδῇς , ὁ παῖς —
sections.translation: What else remains shall be my care , for that child is mine , that thou mayst know it . So I , to do my brother Loxias a service , took up the woven ark and bore it off , and at the threshold of the shrine I have laid the babe ,

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: ἑλικτὸν ἀντίπηγος , ὡς ὁρῷθʼ ὁ παῖς .
sections.translation: after opening the lid of the wicker cradle that the child might be seen .

sections.label: Section 41
sections.original: κυρεῖ δʼ ἅμʼ ἱππεύοντος ἡλίου κύκλῳ
sections.translation: But just as the sun-god was starting forth to run his course , a priestess chanced to enter the god ’ s shrine ; and when her eyes lit upon the tender babe she thought it strange that any Delphian maid

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: λαθραῖον ὠδῖνʼ ἐς θεοῦ ῥῖψαι δόμον ,
sections.translation: should dare to cast her child of shame down at the temple of the god ; wherefore her purpose was to remove him beyond the altar , but from pity she renounced her cruel thought , and the god to help his child did second her pity to save the babe from being cast out . So she took and brought him up ,

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδε Φοῖβον οὐδὲ μητέρʼ ἧς ἔφυ ,
sections.translation: but she knew not that Phoebus was his sire nor of the mother that bare him , nor yet did the child know his parents . While yet he was a child , around the altar that fed him he would ramble at his play , but when he came to man ’ s estate , the Delphians made him treasurer of the god

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: ταμίαν τε πάντων πιστόν , ἐν δʼ ἀνακτόροις
sections.translation: and steward of all his store , and found him true , and so until the present day he leads a holy life in the god ’ s temple . Meantime Creusa , mother of this youth , is wedded to Xuthus ; and thus it came to pass ; a war broke out ’ twixt Athens and the folk of Chalcodon

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: οἳ γῆν ἔχουσʼ Εὐβοῖδα , πολέμιος κλύδων ·
sections.translation: who dwell in the land of Euboea ; and Xuthus took part therein and helped to end it , for which he received the hand of Creusa as his guerdon , albeit he was no native , but an Achaean , sprung from Aeolus , the son of Zeus ; and after many years of wedded life

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: ἄτεκνός ἐστι , καὶ Κρέουσʼ · ὧν οὕνεκα
sections.translation: he and Creusa still are childless ; wherefore they are come to this oracle of Apollo in their desire for offspring . To this end is Loxias guiding their destiny nor hath it escaped his ken , as some suppose . For when Xuthus enters this shrine ,

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: Ξούθῳ τὸν αὑτοῦ παῖδα , καὶ πεφυκέναι
sections.translation: the god will give him his own son and declare that Xuthus is the sire , that so the boy may come to his mother ’ s home and be acknowledged by Creusa , while the marriage of Loxias remains a secret and the child obtains his rights ; and he shall cause him to be called Ion , founder of a realm in Asia ,

sections.label: Section 75
sections.translation: through all the breadth of Hellas . But now will I get me to yon grotto ’ neath the laurel ’ s shade that I may learn what is decreed about the child . For I see the son of Loxias now coming forth to cleanse the gateway in front of the temple

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: δάφνης κλάδοισιν . ὄνομα δʼ , οὗ μέλλει τυχεῖν ,
sections.translation: with boughs of laurel . I greet him first of all the gods by his name Ion which he soon shall bear .

sections.label: Section 82
sections.translation: Lo ! the sun-god is e ’ en now turning towards the earth his chariot-car resplendent ; before yon fire the stars retire

sections.label: Section 85
sections.translation: to night ’ s mysterious gloom from forth the firmament ; the peaks of Parnassus , where no man may set foot , are all ablaze and hail the car of day for mortal ’ s service . Up mounts the smoke of myrrh , offering of the desert ;

sections.label: Section 90
sections.translation: to Phoebus ’ roof ; there on the holy tripod sits the Delphian priestess , chanting to the ears of Hellas in numbers loud , whate ’ er Apollo doth proclaim . Ye Delphians , votaries of Phoebus , away !

sections.label: Section 95
sections.translation: to Castalia ’ s gushing fount as silver clear , and , when ye have bathed you in its waters pure , enter the shrine ; and keep your lips in holy silence that it may be well , careful to utter words of good omen amongst yourselves

sections.label: Section 100
sections.translation: to those who wish to consult the oracle ; while I with laurel-sprays and sacred wreaths and drops of water sprinkled o ’ er the floor will purify the entrance to the shrine of Phoebus ,

sections.label: Section 105
sections.translation: my task each day from childhood ’ s hour ; and with my bow will I put to flight the flocks of feathered fowls that harm his sacred offerings ;

sections.label: Section 110
sections.translation: for here in Phoebus ’ shrine , which nurtured me , I minister , an orphan , fatherless and motherless .

sections.label: Section 1.112
sections.translation: Come , thou tender laurel-shoot , gathered from gardens divine to wait upon the glorious god ,

sections.label: Section 1.115
sections.translation: thou that sweepest clean the altar of Phoebus hard by his shrine , where holy founts , that ever gush with ceaseless flow ,

sections.label: Section 1.120
sections.translation: bedew the myrtle ’ s hallowed spray wherewith I cleanse the temple-floor the livelong day , so soon as the swift sun-god wings his flight on high , in my daily ministration .

sections.label: Section 1.125
sections.translation: Hail Paean , prince of healing ! blest , ah ! doubly blest be thou , child of Latona !

sections.label: Section 1.128
sections.translation: Fair the service that I render to thee , Phoebus , before thy house ,

sections.label: Section 1.130
sections.translation: honouring thy seat of prophecy ; a glorious task I count it , to serve not mortal man but deathless gods ; wherefore I never weary

sections.label: Section 1.135
sections.translation: of performing holy services . Phoebus is to me as the father that begot me , for as such I praise the god that gives me food . Whom I call by that helpful name of father ,

sections.label: Section 1.140
sections.translation: Tis Phoebus , who dwelleth in the temple , Hail Paean , healing god , good luck to thee and blessing , child of Latona !

sections.label: Section 144
sections.translation: My task is nearly done

sections.label: Section 145
sections.translation: of sweeping with the laurel broom , so now from a golden ewer will I sprinkle o ’ er the ground water from Castalia ’ s gushing spring , scattering the liquid dew

sections.label: Section 150
sections.translation: with hands from all defilement free . Oh may I never cease thus to serve Phoebus , or , if I do , may fortune smile upon me !

sections.label: Section 154
sections.translation: Ha ! they come , the feathered tribes ,

sections.label: Section 155
sections.translation: leaving their nests on Parnassus . I forbid ye to settle on the coping or enter the gilded dome . Thou herald of Zeus , that masterest the might of other birds with those talons of thine ,

sections.label: Section 160
sections.translation: once more shall my arrow o ’ ertake thee .

sections.label: Section 161
sections.translation: Lo ! another comes sailing towards the altar , a swan this p269 time ; take thy bright plumes elsewhere ; the lyre that Phoebus tuneth to thy song shall never

sections.label: Section 165
sections.translation: save thee from the bow ; so fly away , and settle at the Delian mere , for if thou wilt not hearken , thy blood shall choke the utterance of thy fair melody .

sections.label: Section 170
sections.translation: Ha ! what new bird comes now ? Does it mean to lodge a nest of dry straw for its brood beneath the gables ? Soon shall my twanging bow drive thee away . Dost not hear me ?

sections.label: Section 175
sections.translation: Away and rear thy young amid the streams of swirling Alpheus , or get thee to the woody Isthmian glen , that Phoebus ’ offerings and his shrine may take no hurt . I am loth to slay ye ,

sections.label: Section 180
sections.translation: ye messengers to mortal man of messages from heaven ; still must I serve Phoebus , to whose tasks I am devoted , nor will I cease to minister to those that give me food .

sections.label: Section 1.184
sections.translation: It is not in holy Athens

sections.label: Section 1.185
sections.translation: only that there are courts of the gods with fine colonnades , and the worship of Apollo , guardian of highways ; but here , too , at the shrine of Loxias , son of Latona , shines the lovely eye of day on faces twain .

sections.label: Section 1.190
sections.translation: Just look at this ! here is the son of Zeus killing with his scimitar of gold the watersnake of Lerna . Do look at him , my friend !

sections.label: Section 1.194
sections.translation: Yes , I see . And close to him stands another

sections.label: Section 1.195
sections.translation: with a blazing torch uplifted ; who is he ? Can this be the warrior Iolaus whose story is told on my broidery , who shares with

sections.label: Section 1.200
sections.translation: the son of Zeus his labours and helps him in the moil ?

sections.label: Section 1.201
sections.translation: Oh ! but look at this ! a man mounted on a winged horse , killing a fire-breathing monster with three bodies .

sections.label: Section 2.205
sections.translation: I am turning my eyes in every direction . Behold the rout of the giants carved on these walls of stone .

sections.label: Section 2.208
sections.translation: Yes , yes , good friends , I am looking .

sections.label: Section 2.210
sections.translation: Dost see her standing over Enceladus brandishing her shield with the Gorgon ’ s head ?

sections.label: Section 2.211
sections.translation: I see Pallas , my own goddess .

sections.label: Section 2.212
sections.translation: Again , dost see the massy thunderbolt all aflame in the far-darting hands of Zeus ?

sections.label: Section 2.215
sections.translation: I do ; ’ tis blasting with its flame Mimas , that deadly foe .

sections.label: Section 2.217
sections.translation: Bromius too , the god of revelry , is slaying another of the sons of Earth with his thyrsus of ivy , never meant for battle .

sections.label: Section 2.219
sections.original: σέ τοι , τὸν παρὰ ναὸν αὐ-
sections.translation: Thou that art stationed by this fane , to thee I do address me ,

sections.label: Section 2.220
sections.translation: may we pass the threshold of these vaults , with our fair white feet ?
